name: First Council of Nicaea
description: Convened by Emperor Constantine. Defined the divinity of Christ against Arianism and produced the Nicene Creed. Established the date of Easter and early canons of church governance.

name: First Council of Constantinople
description: Expanded the Nicene Creed to affirm the divinity of the Holy Spirit. Condemned Apollinarism and Macedonianism.

name: Council of Ephesus
description: Affirmed Mary as Theotokos (God-bearer) against Nestorius. Condemned Nestorianism and affirmed the unity of Christ's person.

name: Council of Chalcedon
description: Defined Christ as one person in two natures (divine and human), 'without confusion, without change, without division, without separation.' The Oriental Orthodox rejected this formulation.

name: Second Council of Constantinople
description: Condemned the Three Chapters to reconcile Chalcedonian and non-Chalcedonian Christians. Reaffirmed Chalcedonian Christology.

name: Third Council of Constantinople
description: Condemned Monothelitism and affirmed that Christ has two wills (divine and human) corresponding to his two natures.

name: Second Council of Nicaea
description: Restored the veneration of icons, distinguishing veneration (dulia) from worship (latria). Ended the first period of iconoclasm.

name: Fourth Lateran Council
description: Formally defined transubstantiation as the doctrine of the Eucharist. Also addressed confession requirements and Trinitarian theology against the Cathars.

name: Council of Trent
description: Counter-Reformation council (1545-1563). Defined Catholic positions on justification, sacraments, scripture and tradition, original sin, and purgatory in response to Protestant challenges.

name: First Vatican Council
description: Defined papal infallibility — the Pope, when speaking ex cathedra on matters of faith and morals, is preserved from error by divine assistance.

name: Second Vatican Council
description: Pastoral council (1962-1965) that reformed liturgy, affirmed religious liberty, engaged ecumenism, and addressed the Church's relationship to the modern world.
